result:
The first light that ever touched your face was not sunlight; it was lamplight in a hospital corridor at three-thirteen in the morning when the power flickered and the backup generator coughed before deciding to live. That single bulb, swinging like an anxious pendulum in the half-dark, created a wet shimmer across your newborn cheekbones, and in that instant you were less a child being born than the world being remade. I know this even though no one told me. I carry the scene the way other people carry scars.

We are born into resonance: a slap, a cry, a heart clacking back into rhythm. The universe is less vast emptiness than it is a drum. Sit still for three hundred seconds and you’ll feel it—cities rattle in their own key, telephone wires hum the slow note of suburbs, inside the body a tambourine of blood. When you close your eyes tonight, try to separate the outside murmur from the inside murmur; you’ll find they’re braided, a single frayed rope lifting you every time you inhale. There is a mercy in that. We are never only where our feet are; we move in chorus with traffic lights, cicada nights, old furnaces sighing relief.

I own a cedar box the size of a paperback. Inside: a ticket stub from a 2003 train ride, some rusted nails I pulled from an abandoned barn’s ribcage, a Polaroid of a girl I loved, her mouth mid-sentence, so alive the emulsion itself seems to inhale. Memory is not filing cabinets; it is an ecosystem. Lay any single object on your tongue and the tongue suddenly remembers not just taste but texture, days, prayings. My grandmother’s apron is gone except for its tomato-sauce ghost on the lid of the Tupperware I inherited. When I open it, I do not see leftovers; I see Naples, 1959, boiling seawater in a dented pot because salt was short and coupons were art.

We speak of time as a river but time is a hall of mirrors. Each moment reflects every other moment, and the further you walk the narrower the hall becomes until you must turn sideways to squeeze between the terrible largeness of yesterday and tomorrow. The trick is to close your eyes and listen for the echo that sounds most like your own voice. That is the mirror you have permission to ignore.

In 1815, after Mount Tambora erupted, 1816 became the year without a summer. Crops failed, horses were slaughtered for food, and Mary Shelley, summering near a lake too cold for swimming, invented the modern Prometheus because if the sun had abandoned the world then monsters felt licensed to rise in its place. I think of this when people say “climate fiction” as if weather were new to narrative. Weather invented fiction; weather is the first author who did not sign its work. Read any diary from any century and sooner or later you will see rain fill the margins, you will trace heat waves in the way handwriting shrinks, ice storms in the merciful spacing between words struggling to keep apart like penguins.

I once tried to photograph silence. I took a camera into an anechoic chamber—those padded mazes that swallow every reflection. Inside, the sound of my own blood was apocalyptic. Click. The photograph came out pure black. At first I thought the shutter had jammed, then I realized the dark was accurate. Silence is the color the mind spills when it has no horizon. I framed the print anyway. It is now the blackest square on my living-room wall, and people often mistake it for a turned-off television. “What’s this show?” they ask. “Documentary,” I reply.

There are places that are not on maps and still you can visit. The Town of Failed Love Letters, population all of us. The Avenue of Maybe Tomorrow, closed for maintenance. The Museum of Forgotten Smells curated in the lungs of any dog who has ever run across a park chasing nothing. All maps lie by omission; they say nothing of the scent of coffee that tiptoes across a river at dawn or the moment a stranger waiting for the same delayed flight hands you the last blueberry muffin and both of you pretend commerce is not happening. I have lived entire novels in those delays—not the flying, the waiting to fly.

My father taught me the economy of small kindnesses: always carry a lighter even if you do not smoke; leave the quarter in the cart at Aldi; memorize one joke clean enough to tell children. These are not philosophies; they are stitches keeping the visible world from unraveling. Larger stitches—tax codes, treaties—earn orations, but small ones weave directly into our daily fabric. Yesterday, a kid on the train offered his seat to a man carrying a plant the size of a teenager. The plant looked embarrassed, the man triumphant, the kid shy. Three stops later the plant was gone but the man’s grin had multiplied itself in four watchers, one of whom later held the door for a woman balancing too many file folders. I followed the grin like blood through capillaries for six blocks before it disappeared inside a bakery. That is enough to keep me walking.

Science keeps bumping into the uncanny. Photons behave differently when watched; place recording devices near erupting volcanos and researchers swear the playback captures voices long after mouths are ash. Once you read about phantom-limb pain you will never again pretend the self ends at the skin. Diaries of climbers lost on K2 mention phantom companions who walk just ahead, offering encouragement in languages the climbers never studied. Here is the secret: We are each haunted houses, and the ghosts are all future versions of us trying to get home.

If you must burn something, burn paperwork, diaries, photographs if no one is named in them. Never burn art someone made for you. Fire reads handwriting like a lover; it laughs at cursive then climbs inside the loops and erases the heart line. I have kept every illustration ever drawn for me on napkins. They are continents of impossible coastlines where “thank you” grows like a fern.

One definition of grace is the quiet moment after hitting “send” on an email containing difficult truth and realizing the world does not immediately erupt. Another is the way asparagus teaches us impermanence: you buy it upright, soldier-green; two days later it bows like monks. Throw nothing away; make soup.

I want to retrieve some infant sense, the synesthesia of crib days when colors tasted like temperature. Blue was cold toast, pink was air you could wear. Adulthood is narrowing that range, insisting red means stop, green mean go—binary dread mutilating spectral wonder. Once a year I sit in a dark closet with a pack of crayons and no labels. I choose based on texture alone: the waxy ridges of magenta feel safer than the powdery glide of violet. My thumbnail scraps the paper and I think this is how a violin feels pressed against a neck. Music is only absent color screaming to be heard.

The oldest story we know is not Gilgamesh; it is a woman gathering berries who turns to warn the child trailing her that the reddest ones are sacred. The child listens, not because danger is absolute but because the warning itself is love given syllable shape. We walk the forest still, choosing certain berries, leaving others, hoping every hunger voiced aloud will become fig and mercy, become lullaby and coastline.

I end, as always, with weather. Today rain smells like copper pennies warmed in a pocket. Yesterday it smelled like the inside of a cardboard shoebox where we once kept glow-in-the-dark stars. Tomorrow’s rain will be new; it will insist on adjectives we have not needed yet. Open your mouth to it. Whatever molecules land will learn your address and return years later disguised as music, chipped paint on a lover’s shoulder, nostalgia in an exhale. This is how the world gets written: one tongue at a time, completely, irreversible, already inside next week’s forecast.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
